subtopic_detail: PCOS first-line management in primary care
stem: A 24-year-old nulliparous woman presents to her GP with 9 months of secondary amenorrhoea, gradual weight gain and increased terminal hair growth on her chin and lower abdomen. She is not currently sexually active, has no plans for pregnancy in the next 2 years, and has no personal or family history of thromboembolism. Her cycles before this episode were always irregular at 6 to 10 weeks apart since menarche at age 14.

data_table.Examination: Mild acne on jawline; Ferriman-Gallwey score 11; no acanthosis; thyroid not palpable
data_table.Investigations: TSH, prolactin, 17-hydroxyprogesterone all within reference range; total testosterone 3.8 nmol/L; LH:FSH ratio 2.6; HbA1c 5.8%; TVS shows 14 antral follicles in one ovary, 16 in the other, ovarian volume 12 mL bilaterally

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Lifestyle modification targeting 5 to 10 percent weight loss plus a combined oral contraceptive for cycle regulation and androgen control
options.rationale: AU PCOS guidance recommends lifestyle as first line, with a COCP added for menstrual regulation and hyperandrogenism when fertility is not currently desired and there is no contraindication. (Source: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG)
source_refs: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg on days 2 to 6 of an induced cycle to restore ovulation and reduce androgen burden
options.rationale: Ovulation induction is reserved for women actively trying to conceive; clomiphene does not address androgenic symptoms and is not appropriate when pregnancy is not desired. (Source: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG)
source_refs: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

options.text: Spironolactone 100 mg daily as monotherapy to suppress androgens, with no other contraceptive cover added
options.rationale: Anti-androgens are teratogenic and must always be combined with reliable contraception in women of reproductive age. (Source: AMH: spironolactone)

options.text: Referral for laparoscopic ovarian drilling as a definitive surgical solution to the underlying ovarian pathology
options.rationale: Ovarian drilling is a second-line ovulation induction technique reserved for clomiphene-resistant women seeking pregnancy, not primary care management of PCOS. (Source: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG)
source_refs: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

options.text: Empiric metformin 500 mg twice daily alone, without lifestyle counselling or contraceptive review at this visit
options.rationale: Metformin is an adjunct that may be added if cycle, weight or metabolic targets are not met with lifestyle; isolated metformin is not first-line and does not provide contraception or androgen control. (Source: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG)
source_refs: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

explanation.summary: Australian-endorsed PCOS care begins with lifestyle change for any woman with elevated BMI, layered with a COCP when fertility is not currently desired to regulate cycles, oppose unopposed oestrogen and reduce androgenic symptoms. Anti-androgens such as spironolactone require reliable contraception because of teratogenicity. Metformin is an adjunct for metabolic or cycle goals not met with lifestyle alone. Ovulation induction agents are deferred until fertility is actively pursued.
key_points: Lifestyle and weight reduction of 5 to 10 percent improves cycle regularity and androgen profile in PCOS
COCP is first-line for cycle regulation and hyperandrogenism when pregnancy is not desired
Spironolactone must be co-prescribed with reliable contraception because of teratogenic risk
explanation.pearls: A combined oral contraceptive plus 5 to 10 percent weight loss over 6 months reduces free testosterone and re-establishes withdrawal bleeds in most women with PCOS without metabolic comorbidity.

sources.label: International evidence-based PCOS guideline 2023, endorsed by RANZCOG

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia with HELLP features at 34 weeks
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 presents to birth suite at 34+5 weeks with 8 hours of right upper quadrant pain, nausea and a frontal headache. Her booking BP was 110/68; today three repeat readings 15 minutes apart give consistent values around 168/112 mmHg. She has had one normal vaginal delivery at term previously and is on no regular medications. The fetus is cephalic, CTG is reactive with no decelerations, and the cervix is 1 cm dilated and 1 cm long.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/112, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Tender right upper quadrant; brisk patellar reflexes; no clonus; no peripheral oedema beyond mild ankle puffiness
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L, platelets 78 x10^9/L, ALT 142 U/L, AST 168 U/L, LDH 720 U/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, urinary protein creatinine ratio 78 mg/mmol, fibrinogen 3.1 g/L, INR 1.0

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral nifedipine 20 mg single dose, no magnesium sulfate, and steroid course before considering delivery in 48 hours
options.rationale: Oral nifedipine alone is inadequate for this BP and omits magnesium; severe pre-eclampsia at 34+5 with HELLP features mandates delivery rather than expectant care for steroid completion. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g/hour, intravenous labetalol titrated to BP under 160/110, and proceed to delivery today
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with HELLP at or beyond 34 weeks is an indication for delivery alongside magnesium for seizure prophylaxis and IV antihypertensive control. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Intramuscular betamethasone 11.4 mg now, intravenous hydralazine boluses, and expectant management to 37 weeks
options.rationale: Expectant care to 37 weeks is unsafe with HELLP features and platelets below 100; steroids do not change the delivery decision at this gestation. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg, oral methyldopa loading, and admission for cardiotocograph surveillance for 24 hours
options.rationale: Loop diuretics are not first-line for severe pre-eclampsia BP control; methyldopa onset is too slow for crisis-range hypertension. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Platelet transfusion targeting count above 100 x10^9/L, vitamin K 10 mg intravenous, and elective caesarean tomorrow morning
options.rationale: HELLP-related thrombocytopenia rarely requires platelet transfusion above 50, INR is normal so vitamin K adds nothing, and delivery should not be delayed overnight. (Source: SOMANZ hypertension in pregnancy guideline)

explanation.summary: HELLP at or beyond 34 weeks crosses the threshold where delivery improves both maternal and neonatal outcomes versus expectant care. Concurrent magnesium sulfate reduces eclampsia risk and intravenous antihypertensive therapy (labetalol or hydralazine) targets sustained BP below 160/110. Mode of delivery follows obstetric indications. Steroids are reasonable if time permits but must not delay delivery for severe features.
key_points: HELLP at >= 34 weeks is an indication for delivery, not expectant care
Magnesium sulfate 4 g load then 1 g/hour for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia
IV labetalol or hydralazine is first-line for crisis-range BP in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: A platelet count below 100 with elevated transaminases above twice the upper limit and LDH above 600 confirms HELLP and prompts delivery at any gestation beyond 34 weeks.

sources.label: RANZCOG statement on intrapartum hypertensive emergencies

subtopic_detail: Primary atonic PPH refractory to first-line oxytocin
stem: A 28-year-old G2P2 is 15 minutes after a spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+2 weeks following a 3-hour second stage. Active management of the third stage included intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU. She has lost an estimated 900 mL of blood with continuing brisk PV bleed. The uterus palpates soft and boggy at the umbilicus despite fundal massage, an IV oxytocin 5 IU bolus and a 40 IU oxytocin infusion already running. The placenta appears complete and the perineum has no significant tear.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58, HR 118, SpO2 99% on 2 L nasal prongs, Temp 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Soft non-contracting uterus, no obvious cervical or vaginal tear on speculum, bladder catheterised and empty
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L on point-of-care, platelets 184 x10^9/L, fibrinogen pending; booking BP 118/72; no personal or family history of hypertension

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Repeat intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU and continue the existing oxytocin infusion at the same rate while awaiting further bleeding
options.rationale: Oxytocin has reached effective receptor saturation; repeating the same agent will not reverse atony and delays definitive second-line therapy. (Source: RANZCOG PPH statement)

options.text: Intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g without adding any further uterotonic agent or escalation of bimanual compression
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is given within 3 hours alongside escalating uterotonics, not as a substitute for a second-line uterotonic in ongoing atony. (Source: WOMAN trial; RANZCOG PPH statement)

options.text: Intramuscular ergometrine 250 micrograms plus intramuscular carboprost 250 micrograms with intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g concurrently
options.rationale: Second-line uterotonics (ergometrine and carboprost) are added when oxytocin fails, in a woman with no hypertension or asthma history; TXA within 3 hours reduces death from bleeding. Continued bimanual compression is essential alongside. (Source: RANZCOG PPH statement)

options.text: Insertion of an intrauterine balloon tamponade device immediately, before any further pharmacological uterotonic escalation is attempted
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is the next step only after pharmacological uterotonics have failed; sequencing matters in the PPH algorithm. (Source: RANZCOG PPH statement)

options.text: Activation of the massive transfusion protocol with 4 units of packed red cells and 2 units of fresh frozen plasma immediately
options.rationale: MTP activation is reserved for ongoing bleeding above 1500 mL or haemodynamic instability not responding to crystalloid and uterotonics. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Refractory atonic PPH after first-line oxytocin escalates to second-line ergometrine and carboprost, with tranexamic acid given within 3 hours of bleed onset. Mechanical measures continue alongside drugs: bimanual compression then balloon tamponade if pharmacology fails. Massive transfusion protocol activates if losses exceed 1500 mL or there is haemodynamic instability despite resuscitation. Ergometrine is avoided in hypertensive disease; carboprost is avoided in asthma.
key_points: Ergometrine and carboprost are the second-line uterotonics after oxytocin fails
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV reduces death from bleeding when given within 3 hours
Balloon tamponade follows failed pharmacological control, not before
explanation.pearls: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of bleed onset reduces death due to bleeding by about a third in PPH per the WOMAN trial.

stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 at 38+4 weeks is admitted in early labour at 4 cm dilation with intact membranes. Twenty minutes after artificial rupture of membranes by the midwife to augment progress, the CTG shows a prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm lasting longer than 3 minutes. On vaginal examination there is a soft pulsatile loop of cord palpable in the vagina, below a high cephalic presenting part. Senior obstetric and anaesthetic registrars and the neonatal team have been called.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 124/74, HR 92, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, vertex high above the spines, palpable pulsating cord loop in vagina
data_table.Investigations: Booking BMI 26, no scar; CTG demonstrates a sustained bradycardia at 70 bpm with no recovery on lateral position change

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Move the woman to theatre for forceps-assisted vaginal delivery at the current cervical dilation as the most expeditious route
options.rationale: Operative vaginal delivery requires full dilatation and engaged head; with the cervix at 4 cm and a high head this is not feasible. (Source: RANZCOG intrapartum care guideline)

options.text: Reduce the cord gently back into the uterine cavity by hand and continue augmentation with oxytocin to expedite labour
options.rationale: Manual cord reduction risks vasospasm and is not recommended; ongoing oxytocin and labour worsens cord compression. (Source: RANZCOG intrapartum care guideline)

options.text: Apply continuous CTG monitoring in the left lateral position and reassess in 10 minutes before deciding on mode of delivery
options.rationale: Cord prolapse with sustained fetal bradycardia is a category 1 emergency; observation delays a definitive decision-to-delivery interval. (Source: RANZCOG intrapartum care guideline)

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord, place the woman in knee-chest or Trendelenburg position, and proceed to category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: Manual elevation of the presenting part and category 1 caesarean within 30 minutes is the AU pathway; positioning offloads the cord while theatre is prepared. (Source: RANZCOG intrapartum care guideline)

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 micrograms subcutaneously for tocolysis and continue intrapartum vaginal birth in the current labour ward bed
options.rationale: Tocolysis is an adjunct only when delivery will be delayed during transfer; it is not a substitute for emergent caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG intrapartum care guideline)

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse with sustained fetal bradycardia is a category 1 obstetric emergency. The immediate steps are to lift the presenting part off the cord manually, position the mother in knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg to offload the cord, and transfer to theatre for a category 1 caesarean with a 30-minute decision-to-delivery interval. Tocolysis is an option only when transfer delays are unavoidable. Avoid handling the cord itself.
key_points: Cord prolapse mandates category 1 caesarean with decision-to-delivery within 30 minutes
Manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord is the first hands-on step
Tocolysis is reserved for transfer delays, not as a definitive measure
explanation.pearls: Manual elevation of the presenting part with the examining hand in the vagina while transferring to theatre maintains cord perfusion until delivery.

subtopic_detail: Shoulder dystocia HELPERR algorithm first manoeuvres
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 40+6 weeks is having a spontaneous vaginal birth. After delivery of the fetal head there is no restitution and the chin retracts against the perineum (turtle sign). Routine downward traction on the next contraction does not deliver the anterior shoulder. The estimated fetal weight at 38 weeks was 4.2 kg, and her previous baby was 4.0 kg. The clock is started by the midwife and an obstetric emergency call is made for theatre, neonatal and anaesthetic backup.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 128/76, HR 102; FHR last 5 minutes 130 bpm with variable decelerations
data_table.Examination: Fetal head delivered, no restitution, turtle sign positive; second stage 35 minutes
data_table.Investigations: GDM diagnosed at 28 weeks, well controlled on diet; HbA1c 5.4% at booking; BMI 32 at booking

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first set of manoeuvres?

options.text: Apply firm fundal pressure as the woman continues to push to drive the anterior shoulder under the symphysis pubis
options.rationale: Fundal pressure worsens shoulder impaction and increases brachial plexus and uterine injury risk; it is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia. (Source: RANZCOG shoulder dystocia statement)

options.text: Proceed immediately to symphysiotomy under local anaesthesia as the first surgical step in this dystocia algorithm
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a last-resort manoeuvre after first and second-line manoeuvres fail; it is not a first step in shoulder dystocia. (Source: RANZCOG shoulder dystocia statement)

options.text: Push the fetal head back into the pelvis (Zavanelli) and prepare for an emergency caesarean section as the first response
options.rationale: Zavanelli is a salvage manoeuvre after exhaustion of internal manoeuvres, not a first-line approach. (Source: RANZCOG shoulder dystocia statement)

options.text: Cut a generous mediolateral episiotomy now to enlarge the bony outlet and then apply downward traction to deliver the anterior shoulder
options.rationale: Shoulder dystocia is a bony, not soft tissue, obstruction; episiotomy alone does not relieve impaction and is reserved to facilitate internal manoeuvres when needed. (Source: RANZCOG shoulder dystocia statement)

options.text: Hyperflex the maternal hips onto the abdomen (McRoberts position) and apply suprapubic pressure on the posterior aspect of the anterior shoulder
options.rationale: McRoberts position plus suprapubic pressure resolves 50 to 60 percent of dystocias and is the first step in the HELPERR algorithm. (Source: RANZCOG shoulder dystocia statement)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is a bony obstruction. The HELPERR algorithm starts with Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts) and Pressure (suprapubic) before progressing to internal manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods screw, posterior arm). Fundal pressure is contraindicated. Zavanelli and symphysiotomy are reserved as salvage. Documentation of manoeuvre order and time intervals is mandated for medico-legal review.
key_points: McRoberts position plus suprapubic pressure resolves about half of shoulder dystocias
Fundal pressure is contraindicated as it worsens impaction
Internal rotational manoeuvres follow if McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail
explanation.pearls: McRoberts hyperflexion alone resolves around a third of dystocias; adding suprapubic pressure over the posterior aspect of the anterior shoulder raises that to 50 to 60 percent.
